Empires & Puzzles Griffex Review: The Purple Mana Manipulator
Is Griffex the master of reality or just a slow purple dude with a cool beard? We dive into his mana manipulation skills to see if he's worth your precious tabards in today's fast-paced meta.
Griffex Review: The Bearded Wizard of Mana Control... or Lack Thereof?
Griffex: The Wizard Who Arrived Late to the Party
Griffex is a 5-star Legendary Dark hero from the

family, originally released in May 2023. He was designed as a "Mana Manipulator," a support hero intended to control the flow of battle by draining enemy mana and boosting his allies. In a game dominated by speed and damage, Griffex took a different approach—a very
approach. While his beard game is S-tier, his impact on the battlefield has been a topic of hot debate since day one.
of Griffex. (Yes, he has a costume now, which basically injects him with super-soldier serum, but we'll get to that later).

Griffex looks like he's about to drop the hottest mixtape of the wizarding world. With a magnificent, gravity-defying purple beard that seemingly flows into the very fabric of reality (or maybe just some magical scrolls), he certainly looks the part of a powerful sage. The runes and floating papers give him an "ancient knowledge" vibe. It's a classic high-fantasy design that says, "I have read many books, and I am very disappointed in your life choices."
Let's be brutally honest: by today's standards (late 2025),
Griffex's base stats are ancient history
This is significantly lower than modern hitters who push 1300+. He hits like a wet noodle.
While decent in 2023, these defensive stats are now "squishy" territory compared to modern tanks boasting 1300+ Defense and 2400+ Health.
Without his costume or heavy Limit Breaking, base Griffex is extremely fragile and likely to vanish before he can even cast his slow special.
, is a double-edged sword wrapped in a slow mana speed package.

A 50% cut for everyone else is decent, but at
, it often feels like "too little, too late." By the time he fires, the enemy has likely already used their specials.
Giving 30% mana to Dark allies is his redeeming quality. It allows him to act as a battery for other slow, devastating purple heroes (like Alfrike or Mortewitch). However, he
, which prevents him from looping his own skill.
With a Level 29 Magic/Styx troop (+20%) and his Druid talent (+4%), he can technically reach 9 tiles in Rush Attack, but in normal play, he's a 12-tile hero.
He deals absolutely no direct damage.

Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Super Elemental family is weird but fun.

The Power Gems are a nice passive damage source, especially in mono teams. Dealing 20 damage per turn doesn't sound like much, but if you have 5 gems on the board, that's 100 unavoidable damage per turn. It adds up in long attrition fights.
This is fantastic for tile damage. Dealing
against Holy enemies means his tiles hit like trucks against yellow tanks. This is arguably his best feature on offense.
Receiving -10% damage from Holy is... okay. It helps him survive a sniper shot, but don't count on it to save him from a modern yellow nuker.
gives him some sustain at the start of the battle. For a Slow hero, any survival tool is welcome to help him live long enough to fire.
Upon release, Griffex was widely considered "underwhelming" or even "trash" by the community. He was a Slow hero who did no damage in a meta that was rapidly speeding up. He found a small niche in
wars and tournaments, but was never a mainstream meta hero.
The game has left base Griffex in the dust. Modern heroes do what he does but faster, with damage, and with better stats. Heroes like
offer mana control at Fast/Average speed while also killing things.
Base Griffex is virtually unplayable in standard Diamond Arena raids or Wars. He is strictly a
or a tile-damage booster for Titans/Mythic Titans.
"I'm you, but stronger and with a cooler hat."
The arrival of his costume (Griffex C1) was a lifesaver.
The costume brings him to modern 2025 standards (1230 Power vs 908).
The mana cut against non-Holy enemies increased to
(Costume) is generally better than Druid for a slow hero, as resisting ailments (like mana stop or silence) is crucial to ensuring he fires.
There is almost no scenario where the base version is superior, except maybe if you desperately need the Druid minion meat shield, but even then, the stat difference is too massive to ignore.
Griffex is a support hero. He needs to survive.
Maximizes his bulk. Since he does no damage with his special, Attack stats only help his tiles (which are already boosted by his passive). Keeping him alive to fire his skill is the #1 priority.
Maximizes the "+100% elemental damage" passive. Great for Mythic Titans or heavy stack offense.
He dies if a stiff breeze hits him.
absolutely shuts down yellow heroes. -100% mana is a hard reset.
The passive makes his tile damage against Holy huge.
+30% mana to Dark allies can instantly charge average/slow teammates.
In Very Fast modes, his skill becomes devastatingly powerful.
His biggest Achilles' heel. In normal mana speed, he is often dead before he does anything.
He relies entirely on his teammates to actually kill the enemy.
If the board is bad, he contributes nothing.
Base version is incredibly squishy by modern standards.
This is his home. At Very Fast speed, he can fire quickly, draining the enemy and charging your heavy hitters.
His passive (+100% weak element damage) makes him a solid choice for high tile damage scores against Yellow Titans.
Mana control is always strong against bosses.
Griffex fires, draining the enemy and giving +30% mana to Alfrike and Mortewitch, who then proceed to delete the enemy team.
Griffex - Panther (or C. Panther) - <Any 3 Fast Purple Hitters>
Use Griffex for the tile damage passive to break a yellow tank, then rely on the faster heroes to clean up.
If a Taunt hero fires before him, he only drains the Taunter (though he still drains all Holy enemies if the Taunter is Holy).
or others who resist ailments or gain mana passively can mitigate his drain.
Note that his skill explicitly says it increases mana of allies
. While previously you could chain multiple Griffexes, newer updates often limit infinite loops.

If you pulled Griffex early on, congrats! He is a great tool for controlling difficult map bosses. Don't worry about his slow speed in PvE; bring mana potions. In raids, try to avoid using him against non-yellow tanks until you have better troops.
Base Griffex is a bench warmer
. He is strictly a niche pick for Rush Attack wars if you lack better purple options. Do not Limit Break the base version unless you have the costume to go with it.
Upon release, the community was harsh. One player noted,
"Seems like a 3rd rate version of Fergus, in a meta where there are a million Fergus counters."
"Flanking Ludwig in rush... forces you to bring anti-mana cut heroes."
"Don't level him. Seriously, don't. He's a total waste of mats and aether."
- A forum user who clearly had a bad experience.

Griffex is a hero with a god-tier beard and a peasant-tier kit (in his base form). He excels at one specific thing—ruining the day of Holy heroes in Rush Attack—and is mediocre at everything else. Without his costume, he is a relic of the past. With his costume, he becomes a formidable mana controller.
Keep him for the costume or the eventual Soul Exchange, but don't expect him to carry your team in 2025 without his fancy new robes.
for modern play. It transforms him from a "D-Grade" hero to an "A-Grade" Rush hero.
A comprehensive review of Griffex, the 5-star Dark hero from the Super Elemental family. We analyze his mana manipulation skills, stats, and viability in the modern Empires & Puzzles meta.
